name: Nicaragua

outbound_official.note: No official Nicaragua government outbound visa-list page located. Nicaragua's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (cancilleria.gob.ni / minrex.gob.ni) website is currently offline (ECONNREFUSED on all fetch attempts as of 2026-06-14). No page on any *.gob.ni domain publishes a list of countries where Nicaraguan ordinary passport holders can travel without a prior consular visa. The inbound visa-policy authority (mint.gob.ni - Dirección General de Migración y Extranjería) publishes only inbound entry rules (who may enter Nicaragua), not outbound travel entitlements for Nicaraguan citizens. Aggregator sources (Henley Passport Index, visaguide.world, etc.) indicate approximately 90–130 visa-free/VOA/eVisa destinations for Nicaraguan passport holders but these are not official government publications and are excluded per source policy. Rely on destination-side data.

visa_required.notes: Nationals of approximately 128 countries not on the visa-free list require a Visa Consultada (Category C), which must be obtained and approved BEFORE travel. NOT a pure e-visa: only the initial authorization step is online (apply via tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni — register with email, submit form, upload passport-style photo and passport biodata scan, await Migration review). Per official-sourced reporting of the Ministry's procedure, once authorization is granted the applicant is notified to attend the nearest Nicaraguan consular office abroad, where the visa is physically STAMPED in the passport ('Una vez otorgada la autorizacion, las autoridades notificaran al solicitante para que acuda a la sede consular de Nicaragua mas cercana en el exterior, donde la visa sera estampada en el pasaporte' — Divergentes / Ministerio del Interior). The electronic authorization alone does not permit entry; an in-person consular visit and physical stamp are required, so this is a consular (visa_required) class, not e_visa. Authorization valid 30 days from notification. Reverted from e_visa on adversarial re-verification 2026-06-29: the cited Consortium Legal page and the official mint.gob.ni page were unreachable (empty body / HTTP 403); corroborating official-sourced reporting (Divergentes quoting Ministerio del Interior, Central Law, articulo66, Infobae) shows a mandatory consular stamp step, refuting the 'fully online, no consulate visit or physical stamp' claim. Category C applies to ~128 nationalities including Cuba, Venezuela, China (ordinary), Iran, India, Mexico, Peru, UAE, Colombia, and most of Africa, Middle East and Central Asia.

visa_policy.notes: Disposición N° 002-2026 (effective February 16, 2026) governs current visa categories. 69 nationalities are visa-free for up to 90 days. All remaining nationalities (approximately 128) must obtain an electronic Visa Consultada (Category C) in advance via tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni — fully online, no consulate visit required, fee-free. Category B (visa on arrival) was eliminated by Disposición 002-2026. All visitors pay a $10 USD tourist card fee on arrival (Ley General de Turismo), except CA-4 nationals (Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras). Diplomatic, official, service, public-affairs (asuntos públicos) and special passport holders maintain exemption where Nicaragua has bilateral migration-facilitation agreements. CA-4 agreement allows citizens of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras and Nicaragua to move freely within those four countries using national ID cards. No newer disposición (003-2026 or later) found as of June 2026. Extension of stay costs $25 USD per additional 30 days (maximum 90 days extra).

visa_types.name: Tarjeta de Turista / Tourist Entry Stamp
visa_types.purpose: Short-term tourism and leisure visits to Nicaragua. Issued as an entry stamp upon arrival.
visa_types.notes: Paid on arrival in cash (USD). Eligible for nationals of 69 visa-exempt countries under Disposición N° 002-2026 (effective Feb 16, 2026). Extendable up to 90 additional days at $25 USD per 30-day period before expiry. CA-4 regional agreement means 90 days counts across all four member states (Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua). Passport must be valid for at least 6 months. Onward/return ticket and proof of funds may be required at border. $42 USD departure tax applies (usually included in airfare). Yellow fever vaccination proof required if arriving from endemic countries.

visa_types.name: Visa Consultada (Categoría C) - Electronic Visa
visa_types.purpose: Pre-authorized electronic entry visa for nationals of approximately 128 countries not on the visa-free list, for short-term tourist or business visits.
visa_types.notes: Required for nationals of approximately 128 countries including Cuba, Venezuela, China (ordinary passport), Iran, India, Mexico, Peru, UAE, Colombia, and most of Africa, Middle East and Central Asia. UPDATED 2026-06-29: The Category C visa is fully electronic — no consulate visit or physical stamp is required. Applications submitted via official portal tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni. Steps: register with valid email, complete form with personal/travel details, upload passport-style photo (front-facing, plain background, no glasses/hats) and passport biodata scan, Migration reviews and sends payment link by email, complete payment, receive electronic authorization notification, download and print. Official fee: nil (Ministry announcement: 'el tramite no tendra ningun costo'). Authorization valid for 30 days from notification date. Visa verifiable at evisa-verifica.mint.gob.ni via reference code or QR code. Sources: Consortium Legal (2026-02-19), Dentons (2026-03-10), Wikipedia Visa policy of Nicaragua. Category B (visa on arrival) was eliminated under Disposición N 002-2026.

visa_types.name: Visa de Negocios / Business Visa
visa_types.purpose: For foreign nationals conducting business activities, attending meetings, negotiations, or trade events in Nicaragua.
visa_types.notes: Nationals from visa-exempt countries may enter for business purposes under the standard 90-day tourist entry stamp (no separate business visa category). Nationals requiring a Visa Consultada (Category C) must obtain pre-authorization specifying business purpose. Nicaragua's Law 761 does not define a standalone business visa separate from the general entry visa framework - business stays within 90 days fall under the tourist/general entry category.

visa_types.name: Visa de Trabajador / Work Visa (Residencia Temporal - Trabajador)
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals to reside and work legally in Nicaragua under an employment contract with a Nicaraguan employer.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley General de Migración y Extranjería No. 761 (Articles 30–32), foreign workers may obtain temporary residency. The employer in Nicaragua typically sponsors the application. Required documents include employment contract, police clearance certificate (apostilled), medical certificate, birth certificate (apostilled), and passport photos. Application filed at the Dirección General de Migración y Extranjería (DGME). Total immigration law work quota: foreign workers generally limited to 10–15% of a company's total workforce per Nicaraguan labor law. Residency card valid 1 year, renewable. Processing 6–8 months approximately.

visa_types.name: Visa de Estudiante / Student Visa (Residencia Temporal - Estudiante)
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals to study at accredited Nicaraguan educational institutions for the duration of their studies.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761, students enrolled in accredited Nicaraguan universities or schools may apply for temporary residency. Required documents include: acceptance letter from recognized Nicaraguan educational institution, proof of financial support, police clearance (apostilled), medical certificate, birth certificate (apostilled). Application submitted to DGME. Renewable annually for duration of studies. Work is generally not permitted on student status.

visa_types.name: Residencia de Pensionado / Retiree Residency
visa_types.purpose: Permanent residency category for foreign retirees receiving a guaranteed monthly pension or retirement income who wish to reside in Nicaragua.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761 Article 30(3), 'Inmigrante Pensionado' category. Minimum requirement: USD 600/month guaranteed pension income (Social Security, retirement fund, annuity, etc.). Age requirement: generally over 40 years old per common practice. Must spend minimum 6 months per year in Nicaragua. Residency card valid 5 years, renewable. Tax exemption on foreign-source income. Path to citizenship after 2 years of legal residency (180+ days/year). Required documents: police clearance (apostilled), medical certificate, birth certificate (apostilled), proof of pension income (bank statements, pension award letter), passport. Administrative costs approximately USD 150–300 plus government fees (residency card: approximately C$18,312 / ~USD 500 for 5 years). Processing approximately 6–8 months. Fee data from expat.com summary of Law 761; official mint.gob.ni/migob.gob.ni currently return 403.

visa_types.name: Residencia de Rentista / Passive Income Residency
visa_types.purpose: Permanent residency for foreign nationals receiving regular passive income (dividends, rental income, investment returns) who wish to reside in Nicaragua.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761 Article 30(4), 'Inmigrante Rentista' category. Minimum income requirement: approximately USD 750–1,250/month from dividends, rental income, or investment returns (figure from expat.com summary; official minimum not confirmed on accessible government pages). No minimum age requirement. Must spend minimum 6 months per year in Nicaragua. Residency card valid 5 years, renewable. Path to citizenship after 2 years legal residency. Required documents same as Pensionado category. Administrative costs approximately USD 150–300 plus government fees.

visa_types.name: Residencia de Inversionista / Investor Residency (Inmigrante con Capital)
visa_types.purpose: Permanent residency for foreign nationals who invest capital in lawful economic activities in Nicaragua.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761 Article 30 'Inmigrante con Capital o Inversionista'. A minimum of USD 30,000 investment is widely cited in third-party sources including PRONicaragua, but this figure is NOT confirmed on accessible official government pages (DGME page returns 403). Eligible sectors include tourism, agribusiness, and services. Requires: MIFIC Certificate of Investment Registration (RUIE), company registration, municipal trading licence, tax clearance (DGI), notarised declaration of income sources, police clearance (apostilled), medical certificate, birth certificate (apostilled). Must make minimum 2 visits per year for ID renewal. Residency card valid 5 years at approximately C$18,312 (~USD 500). Path to citizenship after 2 years legal residency. Processing 6–8 months.

visa_types.name: Visa Diplomática / Diplomatic Visa
visa_types.purpose: Entry and residency for accredited diplomats, embassy staff, and holders of diplomatic or official passports from countries with bilateral agreements with Nicaragua.
visa_types.notes: Disposición N° 002-2026 (Article 2) maintains visa exemption for holders of diplomatic, official, service, public-affairs (asuntos públicos) and special passports from any nationality with which Nicaragua has signed bilateral migration-facilitation agreements. Duration of stay governed by individual bilateral instrument. Confirmed bilateral agreements include: Nicaragua–Peru (signed Lima, June 4, 2004; ratified Decree A.N. 4965, Dec 12, 2006); Nicaragua–Taiwan/ROC; Nicaragua–Sweden (visa suppression convention); UK (La Gaceta No. 54, March 5, 1969). Many additional bilateral instruments likely in force. Accredited resident diplomats hold separate status under Vienna Convention. No maximum stay specified; governed by terms of assignment and bilateral treaty.

visa_types.name: Residencia por Vínculo Familiar / Family Reunification Residency
visa_types.purpose: Temporary or permanent residency for foreign nationals who are immediate family members (spouse, children) of Nicaraguan citizens or legal permanent residents.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761, spouses and minor children of Nicaraguan citizens or permanent residents may apply for residency. Required documents include: marriage certificate or birth certificate proving family relationship (apostilled), Nicaraguan sponsor's identity documents, police clearance (apostilled), medical certificate. Application filed at DGME. Leads to permanent residency and path to naturalization. Administrative costs approximately USD 150–300 plus government fees.

visa_types.name: Tránsito Migratorio / Transit
visa_types.purpose: Allows foreign nationals to pass through Nicaragua in transit to a third country without remaining as tourists or immigrants.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761, transit (tránsito) is a recognized non-immigrant status for travelers passing through Nicaragua. Nationals who are visa-free may transit without separate authorization. Nationals requiring Category C must obtain electronic pre-authorization via tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni in advance even for transit. Proof of onward travel to final destination required. Duration typically up to 72 hours. No dedicated e-transit authorization system.

visa_types.name: Residencia Permanente General / General Permanent Residency
visa_types.purpose: Long-term permanent residency for foreign nationals who have resided legally in Nicaragua for the required period or qualify under other categories defined in Law 761.
visa_types.notes: Under Ley 761 Articles 30–32, various 'Inmigrante' subcategories exist including: Inmigrante Pensionado (retiree), Inmigrante Rentista (passive income), Inmigrante con Capital/Inversionista (investor), Inmigrante Trabajador (worker), Inmigrante por Vínculo Familiar (family ties), and Inmigrante Científico, Técnico o Profesional (skilled professional). Residency card valid 5 years, approximately C$18,312.15 (~USD 500); reduced rate C$9,156.07 for certain subcategories under numerals 1 and 4 of Art. 30, Law 761. After 2 years of legal residency (minimum 180 days/year), applicants may apply for Nicaraguan citizenship. Dual citizenship permitted under Nicaraguan law.

cbi.notes: Nicaragua does not have a formal Citizenship-by-Investment (CBI) program. No official government source confirms a direct citizenship-for-investment pathway. Citizenship may be obtained through naturalization after a period of legal residency, but this is a standard naturalization process, not a CBI program.

programs.name: Inmigrante con Capital o Inversionista (Investor Permanent Residency)
programs.notes: Under Nicaragua's General Migration Law No. 761 and its Regulation (Decree 31-2012), foreigners who invest capital in any lawful economic activity may qualify for permanent residency as 'Inmigrante con Capital o Inversionista'. The official DGME page (migob.gob.ni) lists requirements including a MIFIC Certificate of Investment Registration (RUIE), company registration, municipal trading licence, tax clearance (DGI), and notarised declaration of income sources - but does NOT specify a minimum investment amount in cordobas or USD. The commonly cited $30,000 USD minimum appears in third-party and PRONicaragua materials; it is NOT confirmed on the currently accessible official pages and should not be relied upon. After 2 years of legal residency (reportedly requiring at least 180 days per year in-country), permanent residents may apply for naturalization. Dual citizenship is permitted under Nicaraguan law. Cost of permanent residency card: C$18,312.15 (approx. $500 USD) for 5 years; reduced rate of C$9,156.07 for subcategories under numerals 1 and 4 of Art. 30, Law 761.

agreements: CA-4 (Central America-4 Border Control Agreement - free movement between Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua; national ID card accepted in lieu of passport)

conditional_access.conditions: Disposición N° 002-2026 (Article 2, effective 16 February 2026) maintains visa exemption for holders of diplomatic, official, service, public-affairs (asuntos públicos) and special passports of any nationality with which Nicaragua has migration-facilitation agreements ('con los cuales el gobierno de la República de Nicaragua ha afirmado acuerdos'). Specific confirmed bilateral agreements on file at legislacion.asamblea.gob.ni include: Nicaragua–Peru (signed Lima, 4 June 2004; ratified Decree A.N. 4965, 12 December 2006, published La Gaceta No. 13, 18 January 2007 - covers diplomatic, special, official and service passports); Nicaragua–Taiwan/ROC (diplomatic, official and service passports); Nicaragua–Sweden (visa suppression convention); UK (La Gaceta No. 54, 5 March 1969 - all passport types, superseded for UK nationals by general visa-free grant under Disposición 002-2026). No maximum stay duration is specified in Disposición 002-2026 for this category; conditions revert to individual bilateral agreement terms.

Visa suppression for holders of diplomatic, special, official and service passports under bilateral agreement signed in Lima on 4 June 2004. Ratified by Nicaragua via Decree A.N. No. 4965, approved 12 December 2006, published La Gaceta No. 13 of 18 January 2007. Ordinary Peruvian passport holders require Category C electronic Visa Consultada via tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni under Disposición 002-2026.

validation_notes: Re-validated June 2026 against official mint.gob.ni and migob.gob.ni pages via Firecrawl stealth scrape (WebFetch/direct access returns HTTP 403). Disposition page at mint.gob.ni confirmed accessible (HTTP 200) but contains only scanned images - country list confirmed via Artículo 66 and Prensa Latina which reproduced the full 69-country list verbatim from the official decree.
Corrected Costa Rica note: removed incorrect CA-4 membership claim. Costa Rica is NOT part of the CA-4 agreement (which is Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua only).
Clarified that Category B (visa on arrival) was eliminated by Disposición 002-2026 - not merely updated. Added this to notes.

RBI: Removed the unverified $30,000 USD minimum investment figure. The official DGME page (migob.gob.ni/migracion/tramites/solicitud-de-residencia-permanente/) was successfully scraped via Firecrawl and does NOT state any minimum investment amount. Set min_amount to null.
RBI: Added official residency card cost from official page (C$18,312.15 for 5 years).
Removed duplicate 'migob.gob.ni' entry from official_domains array.
Added 'public_affairs' to the conditional_access passport_types list to match exact wording of Disposición 002-2026 ('pasaportes de asuntos públicos').
Corrected eligible_nationalities in the Peru-specific conditional_access entry from ["Peru"] (string) to ["PER"] (ISO3 code) for consistency.
Added Category C visa email and PDF form source URL to source_urls.

UAE (Emiratos Árabes Unidos) confirmed to be Category C (visa required) by Artículo 66 reproducing the official decree - NOT visa-free. The existing record did not incorrectly list UAE as visa-free; confirmed absent from visa_free list.
program_name and official_url in cbi changed from empty strings to null (semantic correctness).

visa_types field added June 2026: documented all formal visa/immigration categories under Ley 761 and Disposición 002-2026 based on US State Department (travel.state.gov), UK FCDO (gov.uk), Canada (travel.gc.ca), Nicaragua Embassy (embajadadenicaragua.org/pages/pasaporte-y-visas.php), and legal firm summaries of Ley 761 (central-law.com, garciabodan.com, publicaciones.bdsasesores.com). Direct access to mint.gob.ni and migob.gob.ni returned 403 Forbidden during this research session.

Re-validated 2026-06-27 against official mint.gob.ni (returns 403 Forbidden - blocked to automated clients), migob.gob.ni (returns 403), and via WebSearch + secondary sources (Consortium Legal 2026-02-19, Divergentes, Fragomen, Wikipedia Visa policy of Nicaragua, travel-buddy.ai). Key finding: Nicaragua digitized the Category C Visa Consultada process via tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni (launched February-March 2026). The online portal allows applicants to submit, receive approval, and download the authorization electronically (verified by evisa-verifica.mint.gob.ni). Official fee confirmed as zero. Updated visa_types Visa Consultada entry: online=true, fee_usd=0, official_url=tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni, processing_days_min=14, processing_days_max=30. The 69-country visa-free list under Disposicion 002-2026 is confirmed unchanged. No new disposition (003, 004, etc.) found modifying the country lists since February 2026. No ETA or VOA categories exist. No CBI program confirmed.

Re-validated 2026-06-29: Re-fetched official sources. mint.gob.ni domain still returns HTTP 403 to automated clients. migob.gob.ni returns 403. tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni returns 403. inac.gob.ni (hosts official PDF of Disposición 002-2026) returns ECONNREFUSED. WebSearch confirmed: no newer disposición (003-2026 or later) exists as of June 2026. The 69-country visa-free list is confirmed unchanged across multiple independent sources (Wikipedia, VisasNews, Fragomen, Consortium Legal, WebSearch). KEY CORRECTION: The e_visa array has been populated. Multiple sources now consistently confirm the Category C Visa Consultada is fully electronic with no consulate visit required (Consortium Legal 2026-02-19 confirmed: 'No consulate visit or physical stamp is required — the entire process occurs digitally'; Wikipedia also classifies it as e-visa; Dentons 2026-03-10 titled 'Digitizes the Consulted Visa Process'). The previous gap/uncertainty about consulate stamp requirement is resolved: it is a fully self-contained e-visa. visa_types Visa Consultada entry updated: name changed to include 'Electronic Visa', validity_days set to 30 (from notification date), notes updated. One e_visa array entry added covering all ~128 Category C nationalities.
gaps: The official mint.gob.ni Disposición 002-2026 page contains only scanned JPG images - the full text of the decree (including the precise country-by-country lists) cannot be extracted as text from the official source. Country lists verified via third-party sources (Artículo 66, Prensa Latina) that reproduced the official lists verbatim.

The full Category C list (128 nationalities requiring consulted visa) is only partially enumerable from available sources. Confirmed Category C countries include: Afghanistan, Andorra, Antigua and Barbuda, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Bahamas, Bangladesh, Barbados, Benin, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Botswana, Burkina Faso, Burundi, Bhutan, Cape Verde, Cambodia, Cameroon, Chad, Colombia, Comoros, Congo, DR Congo, North Korea, Ivory Coast, Croatia, Djibouti, Egypt, UAE, Eritrea, Slovakia, Slovenia, Estonia, Eswatini, Ethiopia, Fiji, Gabon, Gambia, Georgia, Ghana, Grenada, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Equatorial Guinea, Guyana, Haiti, Cuba, Venezuela, China (ordinary), Iran, India, Indonesia, Iraq, Iceland, Marshall Islands, Mauritius, Jamaica, Jordan, Kenya, Kyrgyzstan, Kiribati, Laos, Lesotho, Lebanon, Liberia, Libya, Liechtenstein, Lithuania, Macedonia, Madagascar, Malawi, Maldives, Mali, Morocco, Mauritania, Mexico, Micronesia, Monaco, Mongolia, Montenegro, Mozambique, Myanmar, Namibia, Nauru, Nepal, Niger, Nigeria, New Zealand, Oman, Pakistan, Palau, Papua New Guinea, Peru, Rwanda, SADR, Central African Republic, Samoa, Saint Kitts and Nevis, San Marino, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Saint Lucia, Sao Tome and Principe, Senegal, Serbia, Seychelles, Sierra Leone, Syria, Somalia, Sri Lanka, Sudan, Suriname, Thailand, Tanzania, Tajikistan, East Timor, Togo, Tonga, Trinidad and Tobago, Tunisia, Turkmenistan, Tuvalu, Ukraine, Uganda, Uzbekistan, Vanuatu, Vietnam, Yemen, Zambia, Zimbabwe.

The $30,000 USD minimum investment for investor residency is widely cited in third-party and PRONicaragua materials, but is not confirmed on any currently accessible official Nicaraguan government page. The official DGME residency page does not state a minimum amount.
No official digital nomad, startup, or fast-track talent visa program found.

Credential-based conditional entry (visa on arrival for holders of valid US/Schengen/EU/UK/Canada visa or residence permit): aggregator/IATA sources suggest this rule may exist for some nationalities but no official Nicaraguan government source (mint.gob.ni, migob.gob.ni) explicitly states this rule as of Disposición 002-2026. NOT recorded pending official source confirmation.

The complete inventory of bilateral diplomatic/service passport visa-waiver agreements Nicaragua has in force could not be fully enumerated. The legislative database (legislacion.asamblea.gob.ni) confirms at minimum: Peru (2004), Taiwan/ROC, Sweden, UK (1969). Many more bilateral instruments likely exist.
No OCI or other diaspora/heritage status rule found for Nicaragua in any official source.
Visa type fees for work, student, and family residency categories not confirmed from official sources - mint.gob.ni and migob.gob.ni both returned 403 during this research session.
No medical visa or working holiday visa programs identified for Nicaragua.

The e-visa (Category C Visa Consultada) fee amount: while the Ministry announced 'no cost', subsequent Consortium Legal reporting mentions a payment link is sent by email. It is unclear whether the zero-fee announcement was permanent or whether fees were later introduced. The official portal (tramitesconsulares.mint.gob.ni) returns 403 Forbidden to automated clients and fee schedule cannot be directly verified.
